questions.question: Explain the concept of Bayesian learning and how it differs from traditional (frequentist) learning approaches.
questions.hint: Focus on the probabilistic framework and uncertainty quantification
keyPoints: Bayesian learning uses Bayes' Theorem to update probability of hypothesis as data is observed
Treats parameters as random variables with probability distributions
Quantifies uncertainty in predictions through probability distributions
Incorporates prior knowledge through prior probability P(H)
Updates beliefs based on observed data to get posterior P(H|D)
Frequentist treats parameters as fixed unknown quantities

questions.question: Define and explain the four key components of Bayes' Theorem: Prior, Likelihood, Posterior, and Evidence.
questions.hint: Remember: Posterior is what we want to compute, Prior is what we know before
keyPoints: Prior P(H): Initial probability of hypothesis before observing data
Likelihood P(D|H): Probability of observing data given hypothesis is true
Posterior P(H|D): Updated probability of hypothesis after observing data
Evidence P(D): Total probability of observing data across all hypotheses
Formula: P(H|D) = [P(D|H) × P(H)] / P(D)

questions.question: What is the assumption of conditional independence in Naïve Bayes classifier? Explain why this assumption is made despite being often unrealistic.
questions.hint: The 'naïve' part comes from this unrealistic but useful assumption
keyPoints: Assumption: Features are conditionally independent given the class label
Simplifies computation of likelihood P(D|H) significantly
Makes learning tractable even with many features
Despite being unrealistic in practice, works well for many classification tasks
Reduces need for large training data to estimate joint distributions

questions.question: List the advantages and limitations of the Naïve Bayes classifier.
keyPoints: Advantages: Simple to implement, fast training and prediction, works well with high-dimensional data, handles missing values, good baseline classifier
Limitations: Assumption of feature independence is often violated, poor probability estimates, zero-frequency problem for unseen feature values, may underfit complex relationships

questions.question: Explain the Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) hypothesis and Maximum Likelihood (ML) hypothesis in Bayesian learning.
questions.hint: Think about when prior knowledge is available vs not
keyPoints: MAP: Hypothesis that maximizes posterior probability P(H|D)
ML: Hypothesis that maximizes likelihood P(D|H), treating all hypotheses as equally likely
MAP considers prior probability, ML does not
MAP = argmax P(H|D) = argmax [P(D|H) × P(H)]
ML = argmax P(D|H)
MAP reduces to ML when prior is uniform

questions.question: Derive Bayes' Theorem from basic probability rules and explain how it is used for classification in the Bayesian optimal classifier.
keyPoints: Start with definition of conditional probability: P(A|B) = P(A∩B)/P(B)
Also P(B|A) = P(A∩B)/P(A)
Therefore P(A∩B) = P(A|B)×P(B) = P(B|A)×P(A)
Rearrange to get Bayes' Theorem: P(A|B) = P(B|A)×P(A)/P(B)
For classification: P(C|D) = P(D|C)×P(C)/P(D)
Bayesian Optimal Classifier predicts class with highest posterior
Select class: argmax P(C|D) = argmax P(D|C)×P(C)

questions.question: Compare and contrast Naïve Bayes classifier with Bayesian Network classifier. Discuss scenarios where each would be more appropriate.
questions.hint: Think about computational complexity and modeling capability
keyPoints: Naïve Bayes: Assumes complete independence between features given class
Bayesian Network: Represents conditional dependencies between features using directed acyclic graph
Naïve Bayes is simpler, faster, needs less data
Bayesian Network can model complex relationships but is computationally expensive
Naïve Bayes suitable when features are roughly independent (text classification)
Bayesian Network suitable when feature dependencies are important (medical diagnosis)
Naïve Bayes often performs surprisingly well despite unrealistic assumption

questions.question: Explain the working of Naïve Bayes classifier for discrete/categorical attributes with a step-by-step procedure.
questions.hint: Focus on the multiplication of conditional probabilities
keyPoints: Step 1: Calculate prior probability P(C) for each class from training data
Step 2: For each feature, calculate conditional probability P(Xi|C) for each value
Step 3: For new instance, calculate posterior for each class: P(C|X) ∝ P(C) × ∏ P(Xi|C)
Step 4: Use Laplace smoothing to handle zero probabilities
Step 5: Select class with highest posterior as prediction
Feature independence assumption simplifies computation of joint likelihood

questions.question: Design a Naïve Bayes classifier for spam email detection. Explain the feature representation, probability estimation, and classification process.
questions.hint: Think of email as a collection of words (features)
keyPoints: Feature representation: Bag-of-words model - presence/absence or frequency of words
Classes: Spam and Non-spam (Ham)
Prior P(Spam) and P(Ham) from training data
Likelihood: P(wordi|Spam) and P(wordi|Ham) - estimate from word frequencies in training emails
For new email: Calculate P(Spam|Email) ∝ P(Spam) × ∏ P(wordi|Spam)
Calculate P(Ham|Email) similarly
Classify as spam if P(Spam|Email) > P(Ham|Email)
Apply Laplace smoothing to handle unseen words

questions.question: Explain how Bayesian learning can be applied to hypothesis testing and model selection. Discuss the role of Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) or Minimum Description Length (MDL).
questions.hint: Think about how to balance model complexity with accuracy
keyPoints: Bayesian model selection compares posterior probabilities of different hypotheses
P(H1|D) vs P(H2|D) - select hypothesis with higher posterior
Posterior proportional to likelihood × prior: P(H|D) ∝ P(D|H) × P(H)
BIC approximates Bayes factor: BIC = -2×log(L) + k×log(n)
MDL principle: Best model minimizes description length
Trade-off between model complexity and goodness of fit
Penalizes complex models to prevent overfitting

questions.question: In a medical diagnostic test for a disease, the following probabilities are given: P(Positive|Disease) = 0.99, P(Positive|No Disease) = 0.05, P(Disease) = 0.01. Using Bayes' Theorem, calculate the probability that a person has the disease given that they tested positive.
questions.hint: Remember to calculate the evidence P(Positive) first
keyPoints: Given: P(D|+) = ?
P(D) = 0.01 (prior), P(+|D) = 0.99 (sensitivity)
P(+|¬D) = 0.05 (false positive rate)
First calculate P(+) = P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)
Apply Bayes: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D)/P(+)

questions.question: A classifier uses Naïve Bayes with three Boolean features (X1, X2, X3) and two classes (C1, C2). Given the following probability estimates from training data, classify the instance X1=true, X2=true, X3=false: P(C1)=0.6, P(C2)=0.4, P(X1=true|C1)=0.8, P(X2=true|C1)=0.5, P(X3=true|C1)=0.7, P(X1=true|C2)=0.4, P(X2=true|C2)=0.9, P(X3=true|C2)=0.6
questions.hint: Don't forget to calculate P(X3=false) = 1 - P(X3=true)
keyPoints: For class C1: P(C1) × P(X1|T|C1) × P(X2=T|C1) × P(X3=F|C1)
For class C2: P(C2) × P(X1=T|C2) × P(X2=T|C2) × P(X3=F|C2)
Since 0.072 > 0.0576, classify as C1

questions.question: Consider a Naïve Bayes classifier for document classification with the following training data: Class 'Sports' (3 documents), Class 'Politics' (2 documents). The word 'election' appears in 0/3 Sports documents and 2/2 Politics documents. The word 'goal' appears in 3/3 Sports and 0/2 Politics. Using Laplace smoothing (α=1), calculate the probability P(election|Sports), P(election|Politics), P(goal|Sports), and P(goal|Politics) for a new document containing only the word 'election'.
questions.hint: Apply Laplace smoothing to avoid zero probabilities
keyPoints: Laplace smoothing formula: P(x|c) = (count(x,c) + α) / (count(c) + α×|V|)
For 'election' in Sports: (0 + 1) / (3 + 1×2) = 1/5 = 0.2
For 'election' in Politics: (2 + 1) / (2 + 1×2) = 3/4 = 0.75
For 'goal' in Politics: (0 + 1) / (2 + 1×2) = 1/4 = 0.25
P(Sports) = 3/5 = 0.6, P(Politics) = 2/5 = 0.4
For document with 'election': P(Sports|election) ∝ 0.6 × 0.2 = 0.12
P(Politics|election) ∝ 0.4 × 0.75 = 0.30
Classify as Politics

questions.question: A weather prediction system uses Naïve Bayes with three binary features: Outlook (Sunny/Cloudy), Humidity (High/Normal), Wind (Strong/Weak). Given training data with prior probabilities P(Sunny)=0.6, P(Cloudy)=0.4, and conditional probabilities: P(Sunny|Sunny)=0.8, P(High|Sunny)=0.7, P(Strong|Sunny)=0.4, P(Sunny|Cloudy)=0.3, P(High|Cloudy)=0.6, P(Strong|Cloudy)=0.7. Calculate the posterior probability for Outlook=Sunny, Humidity=High, Wind=Strong.
questions.hint: Calculate unnormalized scores first, then normalize
keyPoints: For class Sunny: P(S) × P(S|S) × P(H|S) × P(W|S)
For class Cloudy: P(C) × P(S|C) × P(H|C) × P(W|C)
Classify as Sunny
